[Page 1]

PROBLEMS & PARADIGMS Prospects & Overviews

Chromatin Architecture in the Fly: Living without CTCF/Cohesin Loop Extrusion? Alternating Chromatin States Provide a Basis for Domain Architecture in Drosophila Nicholas E. Matthews and Rob White* cracks have started to appear in this picture of conserved genome architecture. TADs are seen as domains of enhanced interaction in 2D genome interaction maps and can be visualized as triangular domains on a linear DNA interaction map as in Figure 1. Analytically, TADs can be defined as discrete regions of DNA containing sequences that are significantly more likely to interact with other DNA sequences within the domain compared to those outside of it. Algorithms can be used to call TAD boundaries; however, the size of TADs identified tends to vary substantially depending on the resolution of the studies, inhibiting a consistent definition and suggesting a hierarchical domain organization; issues that will be further explored in this review. TADs are likely to play key roles in the regulation of gene activity within the genome.[4–7] Here we focus on and review recent work on chromatin organization in Drosophila and compare it to the emerging understanding of mechanisms of mammalian genome packaging.

The organization of the genome into topologically associated domains (TADs) appears to be a fundamental process occurring across a wide range of eukaryote organisms, and it likely plays an important role in providing an architectural foundation for gene regulation. Initial studies emphasized the remarkable parallels between TAD organization in organisms as diverse as Drosophila and mammals. However, whereas CCCTC‐binding factor (CTCF)/cohesin loop extrusion is emerging as a key mechanism for the formation of mammalian topological domains, the genome organization in Drosophila appears to depend primarily on the partitioning of chromatin state domains. Recent work suggesting a fundamental conserved role of chromatin state in building domain architecture is discussed and insights into genome organization from recent studies in Drosophila are considered.

1. Introduction When the first studies on the 3D organization of chromatin using high‐resolution genome‐wide proximity mapping (e.g., Hi‐C) were reported in 2012 they revealed a similar chromatin domain architecture in Drosophila and mammals and suggested a highly conserved fundamental mechanism of genome packaging.[1–3] In both flies and mammals, the genome was seen to be partitioned into a series of domains of enhanced chromatin interaction called topologically associated domains (TADs). However, more recently,

2. How Is Domain Architecture Established across Different Organisms? 2.1. CCCTC‐Binding Factor (CTCF) Plays an Important Role in Mammalian Genome Architecture Following on from the initial investigations identifying the TAD architecture of the genome, subsequent mammalian studies at higher resolution revealed that many interaction domains exhibited a distinct topological signature: a particularly strong “hotspot” of interaction between sequences at the domain boundaries (Figure 1).[8] Such hotspots, present in at least 39% of domains, indicated that these domains had a loop topology with persistent specific interactions between the sequences forming the bases of the loops (loop anchors). Investigation of loop anchor sequences revealed a strong enrichment for binding sites for the insulator protein CTCF and for the cohesin complex. Remarkably, the CTCF sites bounding individual loop domains have a highly preferred convergent orientation. This important observation ties chromatin architecture to specific sequence elements and the pairing of

[Page 2]

Figure 1. Domain topologies in mammals and Drosophila. a,b) In mammals many domains have a loop topology with the specific interaction of convergent CTCF‐binding sites (blue arrows). a) Blue triangles indicate domains of enhanced interaction with an interaction “hotspot” at the apex and black dotted arrows indicate loop anchor interactions. b) Loop domain anchored by the CTCF/cohesin complex (red oval). c,d) In Drosophila, “inactive” domains (blue bar) are flanked by “active” domains (yellow bars). Black arrows in (c) indicate interaction between active domains, generating a “crown” of enhanced interaction above the inactive domain. d) Interaction between active domains forms a loose loop organization.

convergent sites in loop formation indicates that the loop architecture is inherently encoded in the genome sequence. The association of CTCF and cohesin at the loop domain anchors has led to the proposal of a specific molecular mechanism of CTCF/cohesin‐dependent loop extrusion for the generation of loop domains.[9,10] The loop extrusion model proposes that loop‐ extruding factors, such as cohesin, bind to DNA and facilitate loop formation with translocation stopped by boundary factors, such as CTCF.[10] This model is a potential solution to the problem of enhancer–promoter search providing a mechanism through which two distally located regions of DNA can be brought into association. Computational modeling has shown that the dynamic interaction of loop‐extruding and boundary factors can recapitulate TAD patterns consistent with those observed from Hi‐C data.[9–11] The evidence for the cooperative roles of cohesin and CTCF in mediating mammalian loop extrusion is provided by a growing number of depletion and CRISPR‐editing experiments. Depletion of cohesin has been shown to reduce or remove DNA looping;[6,12,13] meanwhile, CTCF depletion as well a deletion or an inversion of CTCF‐binding sites more specifically disrupts domain structure.[13–16] The differential impacts of interfering with cohesin or CTCF action suggest a dynamic interacting role of the two in forming loops and TADs in mammalian systems.[10] 2.2. Drosophila Lacks Evidence for CTCF‐Mediated Loop/ Domain Formation Although evidence is accumulating supporting the relevance of CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion as a key mechanism for domain

formation in mammalian systems,[6,7,9,10,12,17,18] the lack of evidence for this mechanism in Drosophila is beginning to undermine the idea of a fundamental conservation of the processes constructing genome architecture. Despite the initial similarity of genome‐wide domain organization in flies and mammals, higher resolution studies in Drosophila revealed a rather different topological signature in interaction domains. In general, Drosophila domains lack the hotspots indicative of highly localized loop anchor elements. Instead, as illustrated in Figure 1c, many domains exhibit a “crown” of enhanced interaction deriving from the interaction of flanking domains.[19–21] In particular, the interaction of active domains flanking inactive domains may lead to a loose loop topology based on chromatin state domains rather than on precise loop anchor elements. A small number of Drosophila domains with apical interaction hotspots have been identified, but these are found to relate not to CTCF but to interactions between Polycomb silencing complexes.[22] Further evidence against the conservation of CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion is that, although Drosophila contains homologues of CTCF and cohesin–complex components, the specific hallmark of this mechanism, the pairing of convergent CTCF sites at domain boundaries, is lacking.[21] Replication in Drosophila of the analysis demonstrating clear orientation pairing of CTCF sites in mammals shows no preferential orientation bias for CTCF at TAD boundaries (Figure 2). In addition, in contrast to the mammalian situation, the evidence of CTCF and cohesin colocalization in Drosophila is unclear: one study reports little overlap between the binding sites of CTCF and the cohesin subunit stromalin,[23] whereas another study found nearly half of

Figure 2. The bias for convergent CTCF sites at borders of human TADs is not seen in Drosophila. Distribution of numbers of plus strand (blue bars) and minus strand (orange bars). CTCF sites are indicated with respect to TAD borders. a,b) Human data from Rao et al.[8] c,d) Drosophila data from El‐Sharnouby et al.[19] so most likely no role of CTCF in fly domains, but what about cohesin?

CTCF colocalizing with the cohesin subunit Rad21.[24] Furthermore, the homology between mammalian and Drosophila CTCF appears to be restricted to the Zn‐finger domains,[25,26] whereas the interaction between mammalian CTCF and cohesin requires sites in the CTCF C‐terminal domain.[27] CTCF itself appears to have a less general function in Drosophila than in mammals. Whereas the lack of CTCF is embryonic lethal in mammals, in Drosophila CTCF null mutants (completely lacking both maternal and zygotic CTCF) survive past the larval stage and die as late pupae with visible defects associated with Hox gene misexpression.[28] Although Drosophila CTCF is clearly linked to functional regulatory domains in the Hox complexes,[29,30] it is generally only weakly enriched at TAD boundaries.[19,31–33] A recent paper suggests that CTCF may have a specific role in certain differentiated cell types as this study found a stronger enrichment of CTCF at TAD borders in a cell line derived from larval central nervous system than in Kc167 cells originally derived from embryos.[34] In addition to CTCF, Drosophila contains a number of less‐ conserved insulator binding proteins, such as Chromator and boundary element associated factor of 32 kD (BEAF‐32), that are much more highly enriched at TAD boundaries and appear to serve a more general architectural function.[19,31–33]

2.3. Does This Mean Domain Architecture Formation Is Divergent? So if CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion is a general mechanism of domain formation in mammals but not in Drosophila, does this

mean that the basic principles of the seemingly fundamental process of generating the domain architecture of the genome are completely different between mammals and Drosophila? Recent experiments in mammals investigating the effects of depletion of CTCF or cohesin components suggest an alternative view.[6,7,12] Removal of CTCF or cohesin has a strong effect on the loop domains, corresponding to a clear reduction in the interactions at the loop anchor hotspots. However, although loops are lost or disrupted, an underlying domain architecture dependent on chromatin state is preserved or even, in the case of reduced cohesin components, enhanced. The relevance of chromatin state for the nuclear organization of the genome was recognized in the initial low‐ resolution mammalian Hi‐C studies where multi‐megabase regions were segregated into A and B subnuclear compartments correlating with their content of transcriptionally “active” or “inactive” chromatin.[35] Higher resolution studies and the persistent domain structure in the absence of cohesin‐ dependent looping reveals a much finer compartment structure linking the epigenetic landscape of chromatin marks to the domain organization of the genome.[8,12] Interestingly, computational modeling has demonstrated how the combination of epigenetic compartmentalization (A and B compartments) and loop extrusion‐mediated TAD formation recapitulates mammalian Hi‐C results[11] while cohesin and CTCF depletion experiments also support the combined role of these two mechanisms.[6,7,12] In Drosophila, several studies have also revealed a close linkage between chromatin state and the domain architecture of the genome.[1,19,33,36] Thus, while Drosophila domain organization does not generally depend on CTCF/cohesin‐based looping, the underlying

[Page 4]

compartmentalization on the basis of chromatin state may represent a conserved fundamental principle of genome organization in both fly and mammalian systems.

3. Recent Experimental Results from Drosophila Give Fresh Insight 3.1. Alternating Active/Inactive Domains Appears to be Conserved from Mammals to Drosophila Recent work on domain organization in Drosophila has provided insights into how the genome is partitioned into topological domains. Although initially the architecture was described in terms of TADs (“canonical” TADs) separated by “boundaries,” later and higher resolution studies have led to the recognition that the “boundaries” constitute separate domains.[19,21,33] So the overall organization can be seen as an alternating series of two types of domains. The two domain types differ in chromatin state with the “canonical” TADs being enriched in inactive marks and the “boundary” TADs being enriched in active marks. Indeed the interaction domain map shows a tight correspondence with two‐state maps of chromatin state using regions either Depleted or Enriched for H3K27me3 (D and E domains)[19] or using the ratio of interactions in HiChIP datasets for H3K27ac versus H3K27me3 to generate A and B compartments[21] (Figure 3). We will use the A/B compartment nomenclature to encompass both A/B and D/E domains as this nomenclature echoes the mammalian active (A) and inactive (B) compartments. Interestingly, the view of the domain organization as an alternating series of A and B domains fits well with the visualization of chromatin structure seen in the band/interband pattern in polytene chromosomes of the Drosophila salivary glands (Figure 3b). Hi‐C of salivary gland chromosomes shows that they have a similar TAD organization as interphase chromosomes.[36] The B compartments correspond to the compact chromosomal band regions,

which are separated from each other by the structurally distinct, less compact, interband regions.[19,33,37] The strong correlation between maps based on chromatin state and those based on interaction domains from Hi‐C experiments suggested that the generation of the domain structure may depend on the aggregation properties of different chromatin states.[33] However, A and B compartments in Drosophila differ in many properties.[19,21,33] The A compartments are enriched in multiple active histone marks, including H3K36me3, H3K4me3, and H3K27ac, as well as JIL‐1 kinase, which phosphorylates H3S10. They also, like polytene chromosome interbands, show strong enrichment for several insulator‐ binding proteins such as Chromator, BEAF‐32, and Z4/Putzig, for RNA polymerase, and for constitutively active (housekeeping) genes (Figure 4). Which, if any, of these properties are responsible for the construction of structural domains from the A/B genomic regions is a key question.

3.2. A Role for Transcription in Drosophila Domain Formation? The role of transcription in organizing chromatin architecture has been investigated by following the emergence of domain organization in the early Drosophila embryo.[20] Initially, prior to the major onset of zygotic gene activation (ZGA), there is little evidence for interaction domains. However, concomitant with the ZGA, the TAD organization develops and closely follows the time‐course of RNA polymerase binding to sites throughout the genome. This suggested a strong potential link between transcription and domain organization. On the other hand, inhibition of RNA polymerase with α‐amanitin or triptolide prior to ZGA did not prevent the emergence of domain organization. This led the authors to argue against a direct link and to conclude that the emergence of domain architecture is independent of transcription. In contrast, triptolide inhibition of RNA polymerase in Drosophila Kc167 cells decreased intradomain contacts and interaction between

Hi‐C from Kc167 cells,[32] A compartments from Kc167 cells,[21] BEAF‐32 ChIP from Kc167 cells (ModENCODE GSM1535963), housekeeping genes from El‐Sharnouby et al.[19] and the FISH probe from Szabo et al.[38] ChIP, chromatin immunoprecipitation.

compartments.[21] The authors attributed the different effects of triptolide in Kc167 cells versus embryos to the greater degree of inhibition achieved in the cell line versus the embryos. This seems plausible and, although RNA polymerase inhibition in embryos did not prevent the emergence of domain structure, there was a significant reduction in intra‐TAD contacts and a strong effect on the interaction between A compartments as in enhancerthe Kc167 cells. Overall, these experiments support a link promoter interactions as a between active transcription and domain architecture. basis of TADs? The several insulator‐binding proteins that are strongly enriched in A compartments are also potential mediators of domain organization. Insulators have long been proposed as major determinants of the regulatory architecture of the genome.[39] Although binding profiles of architectural proteins such as Chromator, BEAF‐32, and Z4/Putzig show a strong correlation with domain architecture, only BEAF‐32 has been directly tested for a role in domain organization.[32] Knockdown of BEAF‐32 by RNA interference in Drosophila S2 cells showed no change in the global Hi‐C interaction profile. Further investigation will be required to determine the role of these proteins in the establishment of domain organization in the genome. For two proteins, depletion studies have resulted in marked effects on Hi‐C contacts. Knock‐down of the pioneer protein Zelda prior to the ZGA in Drosophila embryos leads to the loss of a subset of TAD boundaries associated with high levels of Zelda pioneer binding.[20] Zelda is required for establishing chromatin accessibility and transcription factor binding during ZGA. This links boundary formation with active chromatin and provides an entry point to investigate which of the downstream consequences of Zelda depletion are key to the effect on TAD boundary formation. Also, depletion of M1BP, a protein associated with the promoters

of constitutively active genes and whose binding site is highly enriched at TAD boundaries, affects Hi‐C contacts with a strong reduction in short‐range interactions.[32] This supports a general link between constitutive transcription and Hi‐C interaction although the effects of M1BP depletion need to be treated with caution as M1BP knockdown also affects the cell cycle, resulting in M‐phase arrest.

3.3. A Hierarchy of Domains? The small size of the Drosophila genome in comparison to mammalian genomes provides a major advantage for the production of high‐resolution interaction maps and recently several groups have exploited this together with the in situ Hi‐C protocol to generate interaction maps with the extremely high ideal for resolution of 200–500 bp.[21,31,32] This has resulted in the metainference identification of many more TADs, particularly in the A application! compartments (Figure 5), creating a problem for the nomenclature and these smaller domains have been labeled “sub‐ TADs” or “mini‐domains.” The sub‐TADs or “mini‐domains” within A compartments provide a new level of domain organization and a fresh link with the process of transcription as the size of these minidomains approaches that of individual transcription units.[21] The minidomain boundaries are strongly associated both with transcription start sites (TSSs) and binding sites for insulator‐binding proteins, such as Chromator, BEAF‐32, and centrosomal protein 190 kD (CP190).[21,31,32] As these minidomains form within regions (e.g., A compartments) of similar chromatin state it seems likely that they are formed

by a different mechanism than the chromatin state segregation of the A and B compartments but how these minidomains arise is currently unknown. Possibilities include that they are generated by looping associated with the process of transcription or by a loop extrusion process potentially similar to the CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion but bounded by BEAF/Chromator/CP190 complexes. Although, as mentioned above, the evidence for colocalization of CTCF and cohesin is weak in Drosophila, there is stronger evidence for cohesin colocalization with CP190,[23] an insulator‐associated protein that does not bind DNA directly but is recruited to insulator complexes by a variety of insulator‐binding proteins including BEAF‐ 32.[40] An intriguing aspect of the BEAF‐32 binding sites is that they are associated with TSSs and particularly with divergent TSSs.[41] This raises the possibility that gene orientation may be relevant to minidomain formation and a potential mechanism could involve transcription‐dependent chromatin supercoiling.[42,43] A recent analysis of minidomains indicates that many represent gene loops with the interaction between the TSS and transcription termination possibly related sites (TTSs).[44] Blocking transcription inhibits these gene to RNApol pushing loops and so does depletion of the Rad21 component of the cohesin? (Mirny study) cohesin complex, implicating cohesin, and potentially cohesin‐mediated loop extrusion, in the formation of gene loop minidomains.

3.4. Super‐Resolution Microscopy Enables Visualization of Domains As mentioned above in the context of polytene chromosome bands, the visualization of chromatin organization provides a powerful complement to the genomic approaches to 3D architecture. Recently, super‐resolution microscopy has been used in combination with in situ fluorescent hybridization to image the organization of specific genome regions in Drosophila interphase cells.[38] The images reveal a series of discrete 3D nanocompartments that correspond with B compartment TADs ( Figure 6a,b). The imaging also confirms the difference in the organization of A and B compartments; while the B compartments are visualized as compressed domains the A compartments adopt a more open configuration (Figure 6c,d). The super‐resolution imaging also supports the subdivision of A compartments into minidomains.

4. Conclusions and Outlook Overall, although, based on the currently available evidence, Drosophila does not appear to make use of the CTCF/cohesin loop extrusion mechanism for the general construction of topological domains in the genome, the compartmental

[Page 7]

Figure 6. Domain structure visualized by FISH and super‐resolution microscopy. Samples were dual labeled for probes covering a 3 Mb region ([a,c] magenta; chrom2L: 9 935 314–12 973 080) and for individual domain probes (green). b) Specific compact domain labeling by B compartment probe. d) A compartment probe (from region indicated by red bar in Figure 4) shows a more extended configuration, consistent with a series of minidomains. Reproduced with permission.[38] Copyright 2018, American Association for the Advancement of Science.

although cohesin does seem to have an effect

organization associated with chromatin state domains is shared between Drosophila and mammals. Having a much more compact genome, Drosophila perhaps does not have the same requirement for the pervasive use of a mechanism facilitating the formation of large‐scale loops. The investigations of domain organization in Drosophila, however, still leave many unanswered questions, for example: 1) The view of genome architecture has evolved with the increasing resolution of Hi‐C analysis, resulting in the identification of a hierarchy of domains including TADs, sub‐TADs, and minidomains. How they relate to each other and whether they are formed by common mechanisms remain unclear. 2) The organization of TADs fits with an alternating series of “active” A compartments and “inactive” B compartments, suggesting that the partitioning of chromatin into these compartments underlies domain formation. However, the molecular mechanisms involved in this partitioning are currently poorly understood and the relative importance of chromatin state, transcriptional activity, and insulator‐ binding proteins is unclear. Inhibition of transcription affects domains structure, but the specific enrichment of housekeeping genes in A compartments raises the question of whether constitutive transcription is particularly relevant for the organization of chromatin domains.

3) While the evidence for CTCF/cohesin‐mediated loop extrusion is mounting in mammalian systems and lacking in Drosophila, loop extrusion could still exist in Drosophila mediated by different molecular machinery. For example, members of the diverse family of insulator‐binding proteins found in Drosophila, or the transcriptional machinery,[10] may act as loop extrusion boundaries as alternatives to CTCF. 4) While mammalian and Drosophila systems are sufficiently characterized to explore conserved and divergent characteristics, evidence remains sparse for other metazoan systems. Exploration of chromatin architecture in other taxa will help to determine to what extent principles of genome architecture are conserved (or not) throughout metazoans and beyond. 5) How the 3D organization derived from population‐based genomic proximity mapping approaches relates to struc- metainference tures present in single cells at any one time is important to clarify. Imaging approaches will be vital for this and the recent applications of electron microscopy tomography and super‐resolution microscopy to chromatin structure[38,45,46] provide promising approaches. For example, a recent study used an array of oligonucleotide probes in DNA‐fluorescence in situ hybridization (DNA‐FISH) to trace the nanoscale DNA architecture at the bithorax complex in single cells in Drosophila embryos.[47] The TAD organization is shown to vary in cells from different positions along the anterior–posterior axis of the embryo, corresponding to the different expression of the individual Hox genes in the bithorax complex. Interestingly, deletions of genetically defined boundaries[48] in the bithorax complex disrupted the TAD organization. These 3–4 kb boundary regions bind both cohesin and CTCF among other proteins, raising the possibility of a role for CTCF/cohesin in this context. 6) The current view of TAD organization is largely static and little good is known about how changes in gene expression dynamically question to target with influence domain structure. Different cell lines have similar polymer [33] simulations domain organization, as would be expected if the domains are largely determined by the constitutive gene‐rich A compartments, but high‐resolution Hi‐C has revealed a distinct domain map in a differentiated cell line.[34] In addition, a microscopy‐based chromosome conformation capture approach has been used to detect changes in the TAD organization upon activation of the sna gene.[49] Further investigation of how domain organization dynamically interacts with gene expression will be important for understanding the regulatory function of chromatin architecture.
